summary: DHH criticizes the Danish Domino's Pizza website for prioritizing flashy experience over utility. The site used a full Flash-based shop metaphor that mimicked the real-world experience of being in a pizza shop, including the waiting. He finds the novelty wore off in seconds, leaving only frustration at a faithful recreation of the worst part of ordering pizza in person.
key_points: Flash-based 'experience' websites prioritize novelty over usability
Real-world metaphors in digital interfaces often import the wrong aspects of the physical experience
Cute design gimmicks lose their appeal almost immediately when they impede function
money_quotes: The last version had the complete shop-metaphor-in-flash going, which was cute for about five seconds.
Then the faithful translation of waiting in the real shop kicked in and you just wanted to get out.
key_insight: Digital interfaces that faithfully replicate real-world experiences often end up importing the frustrations rather than the benefits.
